Description:
Hackathon Day 2 - Session 2 - I learned how to translate technical data findings into executive-level recommendations through structured storytelling and visualization. I managed the final production phase, ensuring our visuals were titled clearly and that our recommendations were grounded in the statistical patterns we uncovered, such as the saturation of "International Movies." Leading the final presentation to the class allowed me to practice translating technical jargon into strategic advice, while making the impact of the data apparent through faceted line charts and percentage-based area graphs.

This has improved my insight into the User Experience (UX) of a presentation, ensuring that the audience can immediately grasp the "so what" of the data. It allows me to understand the difference between an academic exercise and a professional business presentation, including the strategic decision to cut certain goals due to time constraints to maintain quality. These skills enhance my ability to present complex findings to senior leadership, ensuring that findings are converted into actionable business advice rather than just raw numbers.

Hackathon Day 2 - Session 1 - I learned how to transition from an individual contributor to a technical lead by taking responsibility for the "Collate and Interpret" phase of the project. I facilitated a collaborative environment by hosting a live-coding session where I integrated code snippets from multiple team members while troubleshooting technical obstacles, such as identifying that a "Prophet" forecasting code was incompatible with our kernel version. I developed a manual workaround to achieve the trend analysis we required, specifically focusing on the "Year on Year" movement of genre categories to identify that "International Shows" dominate with a 20% share.

This has given me a better understanding of "Technical Conformity" in a team setting and the importance of environment management in data science. It improved my insight into how to identify significant trends—such as the top three movie categories (Dramas, International, Comedies) remaining static for three years—rather than just reporting current totals. In a business context, these skills enhance my ability to pivot when technical tools fail and ensure I can deliver deep, trend-based insights that directly address a competitor's strategy.

Hackathon Day 1 - Session 2 - I learned how to standardize collaborative coding environments by establishing a "Master DataFrame" architecture that ensures team-wide consistency. I moved from individual analysis to a lead integration role, where I created specific "Movie" and "TV" subsets that acted as the universal source for all subsequent team visualizations. I focused on standardizing variable naming conventions and data types across multiple code snippets contributed by peers to prevent merge conflicts and logic errors during the synthesis phase.

This has improved my confidence in managing the "helper data" layer of a project, ensuring that every chart—from genre distribution to title growth—feeds from the same filtered logic. It allows me to understand the difference between isolated analysis and building a scalable, reproducible pipeline that others can easily plug into. This is directly applicable in the workplace when collaborating on shared repositories or notebooks, as it reduces the time spent on troubleshooting divergent datasets and ensures the final report is logically structured and ready for stakeholder presentation.

Hackathon Day 1 - Session 1 - I learned how to initiate a high-pressure data project by framing a business problem and executing a rigorous data engineering workflow on a dataset of over 8,000 Netflix titles. I explored the "Understand the Problem" phase of the analytics lifecycle, translating the CEO’s brief into technical tasks focused on identifying how a competitor could optimize its library. On a technical level, I moved beyond basic cleaning to perform advanced data recovery; this involved using .loc indexing to re-map structured errors (such as the Louis C.K. duration/rating swap) and implementing robust handling for missing values in categorical columns like director and country to prevent them from skewing counts.

This has given me a better understanding of the "heavy lifting" required in the pre-processing stage, which often accounts for the majority of an analyst's time. I practiced feature engineering by using Regex to extract numerical values from strings (e.g., pulling "90" from "90 min") and creating a "Years Since Release" metric to analyse acquisition strategy. These skills enhance my ability to architect multi-layered data structures that support complex queries rather than simply viewing a flat spreadsheet. In the workplace, this ensures that any analysis I provide is built on a "single version of truth" where "Unknown" values are accounted for and dates are standardized to ISO 8601.

I learned how to perform time-series forecasting using non-programmatic tools, specifically Excel and Power BI. I explored the technical differences between Linear Regression (fitting a straight line to data) and Exponential Triple Smoothing (ETS), which accounts for smoothing, trend, and seasonality. Through a practical exercise with a bike-sharing dataset, I learned how to manually configure seasonality parameters—such as setting a 365-day cycle for daily data—to improve forecast accuracy when automated "Auto" detections fail.

This has given me a better understanding of how to interpret 95% confidence intervals and the importance of data volume; I now understand that forecasting an annual trend with only 18 months of data requires human intuition to supplement the algorithm. I have improved my confidence in using the Forecast Sheet feature in Excel and the Analytics pane in Power BI to generate projections. These skills enhance my ability to quickly provide stakeholders with demand forecasts and visual "what-if" scenarios without the need for complex coding, ensuring that business planning is supported by both historic patterns and statistical ranges of uncertainty.

I learned how to implement the numpy.datetime64 and timedelta64 classes to handle dates and times within a high-performance numerical computing context. I explored how NumPy stores dates as 64-bit integers to achieve greater memory efficiency than native Python objects, and learned the technical process of using time unit codes (e.g., [D] for days, [ms] for milliseconds) to define the resolution and scaling of temporal data.

This has given me a better understanding of how to perform array-focused optimizations, such as generating date sequences with np.arange and calculating business days using np.is_busday and np.busday_count. I have improved my confidence in casting between Python, NumPy, and Pandas types, while remaining mindful of the "NaT" (Not a Time) error state and the loss of timezone awareness during conversion. These skills enhance my ability to process large-scale datasets where memory overhead is a concern, allowing me to calculate lead times and productivity metrics across thousands of records efficiently.

I learned how to utilize the Python built-in datetime library to create and manipulate individual date and time objects outside of a dataframe context. I explored the technical differences between naive and aware objects, learning how to use tzinfo and dateutil to embed specific UTC offsets into data to prevent errors when coordinating events across different global regions.

This has given me a better understanding of the ISO 8601 standard, providing a universal framework for representing dates that reduces ambiguity in data exchange. I have improved my insight into using strptime for custom string parsing and timedelta for basic temporal arithmetic, which enhances my ability to configure the initial environment and data types correctly before they are even passed into analytical tools. In the workplace, this ensures that I can handle "raw" timestamp data from varied sources—like server logs or international client exports—ensuring they are calibrated to the correct timezone from the point of ingestion.

I learned how to manipulate and analyze temporal data using the Pandas library in Python, specifically focusing on the handling of datetime objects, periods, and timedeltas. I learned the technical process of parsing date strings, setting datetime indexes, and using frequency-based grouping to aggregate data. By practicing time-based slicing and arithmetic, I discovered how to isolate specific time windows and calculate the duration between events, which is essential for preparing raw data for formal analysis.

This has given me a better understanding of how to manage complex data structures within a data analysis lifecycle. I have improved my confidence in resampling datasets to different frequencies, which allows me to understand the difference between high-frequency noise and long-term trends. These skills enhance my ability to clean and transform organizational data into clear visualisations, ensuring that time-based reports are accurate and logically structured for stakeholders.
Competencies: K14 S10 K3 K7 K13 S2 S4 S5 S13 S14 S15 B3 B4

Description:
I learned the foundational principles of time series analysis, focusing on how to prepare and model data that changes over time. During the workshop, I explored techniques for data pre-processing, including resampling to different time frequencies and applying smoothing methods like moving averages to reduce noise. I learned how to decompose a time series into its constituent parts—trend, seasonality, and residuals—and how to test for stationarity and autocorrelation to determine if a dataset is suitable for forecasting.

This has given me a better understanding of how statistical methodologies like correlation and variance apply specifically to temporal data. I have improved my confidence in using Python libraries such as Pandas and Matplotlib to transform raw timestamps into actionable visualisations. These skills enhance my ability to identify patterns and predict future trends within our organisational datasets, allowing me to provide more accurate forecasts for business planning.

I learned how to choose an appropriate time series forecasting model by analysing the characteristics of the data and understanding the strengths and limitations of ARIMA, SARIMA, and SARIMAX models. I developed a clearer understanding that ARIMA is best suited for non-seasonal time series data that can be made stationary through differencing, while SARIMA extends ARIMA to explicitly model recurring seasonal patterns.

I also learned that SARIMAX further extends SARIMA by incorporating exogenous variables, allowing external factors such as marketing activity, weather conditions, or major events to be included as regressors. This makes SARIMAX particularly suitable for real-world scenarios where the target variable is influenced by factors beyond its own historical values.

The learning emphasised the importance of performing key checks before selecting a model, including assessing stationarity using statistical tests such as the Augmented Dickey-Fuller test, identifying seasonality through decomposition or autocorrelation analysis, and evaluating whether external variables are relevant. I also learned that model complexity should be balanced with interpretability, as simpler models can often perform just as effectively when the data does not warrant added complexity.

In addition, I explored common mistakes in time series modelling, such as ignoring underlying data patterns, overfitting, neglecting model assumptions, and failing to account for external influences. Understanding these pitfalls has reinforced the importance of exploratory data analysis, assumption checking, and validation techniques such as cross-validation.

Finally, I reviewed practical recommendations for applying these models to real-world use cases, including retail sales forecasting, electricity demand forecasting, stock price prediction, and website traffic analysis. This has improved my ability to select and justify appropriate forecasting models based on data behaviour, business context, and analytical objectives.

I learned about the ARIMA family of time series forecasting models, including ARIMA, SARIMA, and SARIMAX, and how they are used to model different types of temporal patterns. I developed an understanding of how differencing is applied to transform non-stationary time series data into stationary data by removing trends and seasonality, including both first-order differencing and seasonal differencing.

I learned how ARIMA models are structured using autoregressive (AR), differencing (I), and moving average (MA) components, and how SARIMA extends ARIMA by explicitly modelling seasonal patterns. I also learned that SARIMAX further extends this approach by allowing the inclusion of exogenous variables, enabling the model to incorporate external factors such as economic indicators, weather data, or marketing activity.

In addition, I explored the strengths and weaknesses of each model type. I learned that ARIMA models are best suited for short-term forecasting with stationary or differenced data, while SARIMA is more appropriate when strong and consistent seasonal patterns exist. SARIMAX is useful for complex real-world scenarios where external variables influence the time series, but it introduces additional complexity and risk of overfitting.

I also learned about the key assumptions underlying ARIMA-based models, including stationarity, linearity, independence of errors, and the absence of multicollinearity when using exogenous variables. Understanding these assumptions helps ensure that forecasts are valid and that residuals behave as stationary white noise.

This learning has improved my ability to select appropriate time series models based on data characteristics, understand model limitations, and apply differencing and diagnostics to build reliable and interpretable forecasts.

I learned about Facebook’s Prophet forecasting model and how it is designed to handle complex real-world time series data. I developed an understanding of Prophet’s core components, including trend, seasonality, and the incorporation of holidays and events, which allows it to model non-linear growth patterns and recurring effects more flexibly than traditional statistical models.

I learned how Prophet requires data to be structured in a specific time series format using timestamp (ds) and value (y) columns, and how it generates forecasts alongside confidence intervals to quantify uncertainty in predictions.

In addition, I learned that Prophet is particularly well-suited for data with long-term trends, multiple seasonal patterns, missing values, and outliers, making it useful for business forecasting scenarios such as demand, sales, or website traffic. I also learned about its limitations, including situations where Prophet may be excessive, such as when data is stable, short-term forecasts are required, or when no meaningful trend or seasonality is present.

This learning improves my ability to evaluate advanced forecasting tools, select appropriate models based on data complexity, and balance model flexibility against simplicity to ensure reliable and interpretable forecasts.

I learned how exponential smoothing techniques are used for time series forecasting and how the choice of method depends on the structure of the data. I developed an understanding of when exponential smoothing is appropriate, particularly for data streams that are stable over time and require quick, computationally efficient forecasting.

I learned the differences between key exponential smoothing models, including Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES) for data with no trend or seasonality, Holt’s Linear Trend Model for data with a trend but no seasonality, and the Holt-Winters Seasonal Model for data containing both trend and seasonal components.

In addition, I learned the limitations of exponential smoothing and recognised scenarios where it is not suitable, such as when data fluctuates unpredictably or exhibits frequent structural changes. This helps ensure that appropriate forecasting techniques are selected based on data behaviour rather than convenience.

This learning improves my ability to select, justify, and apply forecasting models appropriately, supporting more reliable predictions and better data-driven decision-making.

I developed a solid understanding of confidence intervals and their role in quantifying uncertainty in statistical estimates. I learned how confidence intervals provide a range of plausible values for unknown population parameters based on sample data, rather than a single point estimate.

I also learned how confidence intervals are constructed using sample statistics, standard error, confidence levels, and appropriate probability distributions, including the normal distribution for large samples and the t-distribution for smaller samples.

In addition, I explored how confidence intervals are applied within time series forecasting to express uncertainty around predictions, improving the reliability and interpretability of forecasts. I gained awareness of common misinterpretations, such as confusing confidence intervals with prediction intervals or incorrectly interpreting confidence levels.

This learning enhances my ability to communicate statistical results clearly, assess the reliability of estimates, and support informed, data-driven decision-making across analytical and business contexts.
Competencies: B5 B4 K14 S10 K13 S7

Description:
I developed a deeper understanding of stationarity in time series data and why it is a critical assumption for many forecasting models. I learned how a stationary time series maintains constant statistical properties, such as mean, variance and autocorrelation, and why non-stationary data can lead to unreliable predictions.

I also learned how to assess stationarity using formal statistical tests, including the Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test and the Kwiatkowski-Phillips-Schmidt-Shin (KPSS) test. This included understanding the differing null hypotheses of each test and how they provide complementary perspectives when used together.

In addition, I learned how the outcomes of these tests inform decisions about data transformation, such as differencing, prior to applying forecasting models. This learning improves my ability to critically assess the suitability of time series data for analysis and supports more robust, evidence-based decision-making in a business context.

I learned the main approaches to time series forecasting, including the differences between statistical methods and machine learning methods, and the types of problems each approach is best suited to. I gained an understanding of commonly used forecasting techniques such as moving averages, exponential smoothing and ARIMA models, as well as how machine learning models can be used to capture more complex or non-linear patterns in time series data.

I also learned the end-to-end framework for time series forecasting, from data collection and preprocessing through to model selection, training, evaluation and refinement. This improved my understanding of why model evaluation is a critical step and how it supports informed decision-making.

In addition, I learned how evaluation metrics such as MSE, RMSE, MAE and MAPE are used to assess forecasting accuracy, and how different metrics can be interpreted depending on the business context. This learning helps me understand how to assess the reliability of forecasts in the workplace and communicate model performance clearly to stakeholders.

I learned how correlation can be used in time series analysis to measure the strength and direction of the relationship between variables, and how this can support understanding of how different time series move together over time. I gained an understanding of how shifting time series data allows for alignment of datasets, identification of lagged effects, and the creation of lag features for predictive analysis.

I also learned how autocorrelation measures the relationship between a time series and its past values, and how this can be used to identify repeating patterns such as seasonality. In addition, I gained an introduction to the concept of stationarity, including the importance of constant mean, variance and autocorrelation when preparing data for forecasting models.

This learning has improved my understanding of when time series data is suitable for modelling and how techniques such as differencing can be used to transform non-stationary data into a stationary form. In the workplace, these concepts support more reliable analysis and help ensure that forecasting approaches are based on appropriate data assumptions.

I learned how time series data can be reshaped using resampling and smoothing techniques to make it more suitable for analysis. I gained an understanding of how resampling changes the frequency of observations, including the difference between upsampling and downsampling, and when each approach is appropriate depending on the analytical requirement.

I also learned how interpolation and aggregation methods can be used during resampling to either estimate missing values or summarise data across larger time intervals. In addition, I gained an introduction to smoothing techniques, including moving averages and exponential smoothing, and how these help reduce noise and highlight underlying trends in time series data.

This learning has improved my ability to prepare and structure time-based datasets in the workplace, allowing me to align data with business reporting needs, reduce short-term volatility, and better support trend analysis and forecasting activities.
Competencies: S15 S10 K3 K11 K13 S2 B2 B4

Description:
I learned how date and time data must be represented in a consistent and unambiguous format to support time series analysis, and gained an introduction to the ISO 8601 standard for representing dates, times and time zones. I improved my understanding of why different regional date formats can cause ambiguity and how standardisation supports reliable data processing in analytical tools such as Python.

I also learned how dates and times can be parsed, converted and manipulated using Python libraries including datetime, dateutil, pytz and Pandas. This has given me a better understanding of how to convert raw date strings into usable datetime objects, handle missing or ambiguous dates, and work with time zone–aware and time zone–naive timestamps.

These skills enhance my ability to prepare and clean time-based data in the workplace, ensuring datasets are structured correctly for time series analysis, visualisation and forecasting across different systems and geographic regions.
Competencies: S15 B1 B4 K6 K3 K8 K11 S2 S6

Description:
I learned the difference between additive and multiplicative time series models and how the relationship between trend, seasonality and noise affects the structure of a time series. I gained an understanding of when an additive model is appropriate, where seasonal variation remains relatively constant, and when a multiplicative model should be used, where seasonal effects change proportionally with the level of the data.

This learning has improved my insight into how visual inspection of time series data can inform the choice of model, and how selecting the correct model impacts the accuracy of forecasting and interpretation. I also learned how additive and multiplicative trends relate to linear and exponential growth patterns.

These concepts enhance my ability to assess time-based datasets in the workplace and choose an appropriate analytical approach when working with business data such as sales, demand or customer activity over time.

I learned how time series data can be decomposed into its core components: trend, seasonality and noise, and how each component represents a different underlying pattern within time-based data. I gained an understanding of how trends can take different forms, including linear, quadratic and exponential, and how these trends describe long-term movement within a dataset.

I also learned how seasonal patterns occur at fixed and predictable intervals, and how separating seasonality from trend and noise helps to better understand recurring behaviour within the data. This learning improved my insight into how residuals represent unexplained variation that cannot be attributed to trend or seasonality.

This has given me a better understanding of how decomposing a time series supports forecasting, anomaly detection and decision-making. In the workplace, these concepts help me interpret time-based performance data more accurately and understand which underlying factors are driving observed changes over time.

I learned the fundamentals of time series data and time series analysis, including how data points are recorded in chronological order and why the time component is critical for analysis. I gained an introduction to key concepts such as trends, seasonality, cyclic patterns and temporal dependencies, and learned how these differ from standard machine learning approaches where observations are treated as independent.

This learning has given me a better understanding of when time series techniques are appropriate and when time-related variables may not add value. I also learned how visualisation techniques such as line charts and other time series plots can be used to explore patterns, identify anomalies, and communicate insights clearly.

These concepts improve my confidence in analysing time-based datasets in the workplace, such as sales, customer activity or operational metrics, and help me understand how time series forecasting can be applied to support prediction and decision-making.

I learned how to create and manage isolated Python environments using the venv module within Visual Studio Code. I learned the technical commands to initialize a virtual environment, activate it via the terminal, and configure VS Code to recognize the specific interpreter associated with that environment. I also learned how to use a requirements.txt file to document and install specific library versions, ensuring that my project dependencies remain consistent and do not conflict with other global Python installations.

This has given me a better understanding of how to maintain a professional and secure development environment by preventing package version conflicts. This has improved my confidence in setting up a clean workspace for new projects, which allows me to understand the difference between local and global configurations. These skills enhance my ability to collaborate with others, as I can now provide a reproducible environment that ensures my analysis scripts run correctly on different machines within the business.
Competencies: S15 B2 K2 B1 S1 K11

Description:
I learned how to utilize the pivot_table function in Pandas to summarize and aggregate complex datasets. I learned how to define indexes, columns, and values to reshape data, and how to apply different aggregation functions—such as mean, sum, and count—to extract specific insights from multi-dimensional data. I also gained an introduction to handling missing values during the pivoting process and using "margins" to calculate totals across rows and columns.

This has given me a better understanding of how to transform long-format data into a wide-format summary, which simplifies the comparison of different variables. This has improved my confidence in performing exploratory data analysis without needing to write complex loops. These skills enhance my ability to quickly generate summary reports from raw organizational data, allowing me to identify key performance indicators and trends that support business decision-making.

I learned how to enhance the user experience by implementing activity indicators and progress notifications using pn.indicators and the pn.state.notifications module. I explored how to provide visual feedback during long-running asynchronous tasks—such as data processing or model training—by utilizing the LoadingSpinner and the Tqdm progress bar wrapper. Additionally, I learned how to use the pn.state.notifications system to push real-time alerts (success, info, warning, or error) to the user, ensuring the application communicates its internal state clearly during interaction.

This has given me a better understanding of the "human-in-the-loop" design requirements for data-heavy applications. Allows me to understand the difference between a "frozen" interface and one that remains responsive and communicative while background logic is executing. These skills enhance my ability to build user-friendly internal tools where stakeholders are kept informed of an operation's status, significantly reducing the likelihood of accidental multiple submissions or user frustration during high-latency processes.

I learned the technical mechanics of the Panel application execution lifecycle and how to implement performance optimizations using pn.cache. I explored the distinction between module-level execution (shared across all user sessions) and script-level execution (unique to each session), and practiced using the @pn.cache decorator to store and reuse the results of expensive operations. By applying this to both data loading simulations and interactive slider-bound functions, I observed how caching transforms high-latency tasks into instantaneous UI updates once the initial computation is stored.

This has given me a better understanding of how Panel manages state and memory across multiple browser tabs and users. Allows me to understand the difference between global objects defined in external modules and session-specific objects defined within the main application script. These skills enhance my ability to build efficient, production-ready dashboards that can handle large datasets or complex algorithms without degrading the user experience or overloading server resources during high traffic.

I learned how to utilise CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) to perform granular visual adjustments to individual Panel components through the styles and stylesheets parameters. I explored how to pass Python dictionaries to the styles argument to modify container properties—such as border-radius, box-shadow, and padding—and I practiced using raw CSS strings within stylesheets to target and style internal HTML elements (like nested div tags) that are otherwise inaccessible through high-level parameters.

This has given me a better understanding of the distinction between styling a component's outer wrapper and its internal DOM structure. Allows me to understand the difference between using pre-set design frameworks and implementing custom brand-specific styles to meet precise aesthetic requirements. These skills enhance my ability to create bespoke data visualizations and indicators that stand out, ensuring that critical data points—like wind speed metrics—are highlighted effectively for the end-user.

I learned how to apply high-level UI frameworks to data applications using the design parameter within pn.extension. I explored the differences between major design languages, specifically "Material" for clean Google-inspired interfaces, "Bootstrap" for traditional responsive layouts, and "Fast" for modern Microsoft-based aesthetics. I also practiced implementing global theme toggles, such as switching between "default" and "dark" modes, to ensure consistent visual styling across all widgets and containers.

This has given me a better understanding of how to decouple the functional logic of an application from its visual presentation layer. Allows me to understand the difference between "native" Bokeh styling and higher-level CSS frameworks that offer a more polished, consumer-grade look. These skills enhance my ability to rapidly prototype tools that meet internal brand guidelines and improve user accessibility without the need for manual CSS overrides or deep front-end development knowledge.

I learned how to utilize the pn.template namespace to deploy professional, multi-area application structures featuring headers, sidebars, and main content zones. I explored the implementation of the FastListTemplate, gaining insight into how to configure global application properties such as the title, accent colors for branding, and the main_layout behavior. Additionally, I learned how to handle theme persistence by using pn.config.theme to dynamically synchronize the styling of underlying visualization libraries, like Altair, with the user's selected light or dark mode.

This has given me a better understanding of the architectural requirements for transitioning from a collection of individual widgets to a production-ready data application. Allows me to understand the difference between basic layout containers (Column/Row) and high-level templates that provide built-in functionality like theme toggles and responsive navigation drawers. These skills enhance my ability to deliver polished, "app-like" interfaces in VS Code that adhere to professional design standards without requiring extensive front-end web development knowledge.

I learned how to create dynamic, reactive applications using the pn.bind method to link widget values directly to Python functions. I explored several technical reactivity patterns, including the use of value_throttled to prevent excessive function execution, the implementation of "submit-on-click" logic for forms, and the use of the watch=True parameter to trigger side effects such as UI state changes. I also gained an introduction to asynchronous programming using asyncio and threading to prevent long-running, blocking tasks from freezing the user interface.

This has given me a better understanding of the difference between immediate reactivity and manual submission patterns in data tool design. Allows me to understand the difference between updating a whole layout versus passing a bound function as a reference to specific parameters for optimized performance. These skills enhance my ability to build stable internal utilities where I can manage data processing tasks—such as a calculation or a data fetch—while maintaining a responsive environment for the user.

I learned how to implement interactive user input components within the pn.widgets namespace, specifically focusing on Button, TextInput, and Select elements. I explored how to configure functional parameters such as the value property for data capture, placeholder text for user guidance, and max_length constraints to ensure data entry remains within specified technical limits.

This has given me a better understanding of how different widget types, such as RadioButtonGroup versus a standard Select dropdown, alter the user interface footprint and interaction flow. Allows me to understand the difference between passive data display and active data capture, including how to manage component states via the disabled parameter. These skills enhance my readiness to build basic interactive data tools where I can apply these widgets to allow colleagues to filter datasets or trigger specific analysis scripts manually.

I learned how to manipulate the positioning of dashboard components and application of UI layout aesthetics, focusing on content alignment, spacing, and margin management within the Panel framework. This is by using the align parameter (supporting both single values and 2-tuples for horizontal/vertical control), the Spacer component for dynamic distribution of space, and margin settings for pixel-perfect fine-tuning. I practiced these techniques by centering interactive elements like buttons and cards within fixed-size containers, and I explored how to use spacers to create proportional vertical gaps between dashboard sections.

This has given me a better understanding of the visual hierarchy and balance required to create professional-grade data interfaces. This has improved my confidence in moving beyond default "top-left" layouts to more sophisticated, centered, and balanced designs. Allows me to understand the difference between using programmatic alignment (which scales) and hard-coded margins (which provide fixed spacing). These skills enhance my ability to design intuitive dashboards where key controls and call-to-action buttons are prominently placed and logically separated from other data elements.

I learned how to manage the spatial behavior of dashboard components by implementing different sizing_mode configurations, such as stretch_width, stretch_height, and stretch_both. I did this through a technical exploration of responsive design principles and component sizing constraints of the Panel framework. I explored how to set strict boundary constraints using min_width and max_width to ensure readability on various screen sizes, and I practiced using the FlexBox layout to create fluid, "wrap-around" designs. Additionally, I learned how to use pn.Param to create interactive fine-tuning widgets, allowing for real-time adjustments of UI parameters without manual code restarts.

This has given me a better understanding of the difference between inherent, absolute, and responsive sizing in web-based data applications. This has improved my confidence in building dashboards that look professional on both large desktop monitors and smaller laptop screens. Allows me to understand the difference between a "fixed" layout that might clip content and a "responsive" layout that adapts to user behaviour. These skills enhance my ability to deliver high-quality data products that adhere to modern web accessibility and usability standards.

I learned how to arrange dashboard elements both vertically and horizontally using layout containers, through the application of the pn.Column and pn.Row namespaces to structure and organize data dashboard components effectively. I explored the "list-like" nature of these layouts, which allows for programmatic manipulation such as indexing (component[0]), appending, and reordering elements. I also practiced nesting layouts—placing a Row inside a Column—to create complex, professional-grade visual hierarchies that combine markdown headers, interactive widgets like buttons, and data visualizations.

This has given me a better understanding of the difference between simply displaying objects and architecting an organized data application. These skills enhance my ability to build responsive reports where the sizing mode and arrangement adapt logically to the viewer's screen.

I learned how to implement specialized high-level components such as the Number indicator and the Trend indicator to provide immediate visual context to data. I explored how to apply conditional formatting through the colors parameter - allowing the UI to change dynamically based on data thresholds (e.g., green vs. red for wind speeds) - and how to supply dictionary-based time-series data to the Trend component to visualize percentage changes and Sparklines. This has improved my confidence in designing dashboards that go beyond simple tables and plots to include summarized performance metrics and allows me to understand the difference between static data reporting and dynamic status monitoring.

I learned how to explicitly define display components using specific Panes such as pn.pane.Markdown, pn.pane.Vega, and pn.pane.DataFrame rather than relying on automated heuristic selection. I did this during a technical deep-dive into the pn.pane namespace within the Panel library to achieve precise control over data rendering. I explored the importance of the Component Gallery for navigating specialized display types and practiced incorporating metadata-driven UI elements like pn.pane.Alert for information callouts.

This has given me a better understanding of the trade-offs between the ease of use provided by generic functions and the performance benefits of explicit object typing. These skills enhance my ability to build high-performance data applications that handle complex, multi-modal data (audio, images, and plots) with greater stability.

I learned how to utilize the pn.panel() function to programmatically convert diverse Python objects—including strings, DataFrames, and Altair plots—into viewable dashboard components. I explored the use of pn.extension() to load necessary JavaScript dependencies (like Vega for Altair) and practiced using .servable() to transition components from a local development environment to a web-hosted application.
This has given me a better understanding of the heuristic algorithms used by dashboarding frameworks to determine the best display format for different data structures. This has improved my confidence in using VS Code for front-end data representation and allows me to understand the difference between standard notebook rendering and serving an interactive app. These skills enhance my ability to create functional prototypes that bridge the gap between raw data analysis and stakeholder-accessible interfaces.

I learned how to configure my VS Code environment to support the Panel library and gained an introduction to the basic workflow of serving a Python script as a local web app. I learned the specific roles of pn.extension() for environment configuration and .servable() for identifying displayable components. I also improved my insight into using the terminal to run a local Bokeh server and how to use the --dev flag for real-time updates.

This has given me a better understanding of how Python code can be transitioned from a static script into a browser-based format. This improved confidence in my local setup allows me to understand the difference between standard code execution and server-side rendering. These fundamental skills enhance my ability to test small snippets of interactive code locally before attempting to build more complex data visualisations.

I attended a workshop and learned how to extend my data visualization capabilities by performing a deep-dive analysis on the Beatles discography dataset using Seaborn and Matplotlib. I explored the practical application of statistical visualization techniques, such as using KDE (Kernel Density Estimate) plots to understand feature distributions, Pairplots to identify multi-variable relationships, and Regplots to model linear regressions between song attributes. I gained an introduction to the "Human-in-the-loop" aspect of data design, specifically how adjusting colour palettes, font sizes, and axis labels can significantly reduce the cognitive load on the audience and make the insights more accessible.

This has given me a better understanding of the iterative nature of the Data Analytics Lifecycle, specifically the "Visualize" and "Evaluate" stages. Allows me to understand the difference between a default chart generated by code and a polished, professional visualization that is tailored to a specific stakeholder's domain context. These skills enhance my ability to select the most appropriate data tools - comparing the rapid plotting of Pandas with the high-level aesthetics of Seaborn - to achieve an optimum outcome that is both analytically rigorous and visually intuitive.

I learned about the diverse landscape of dashboarding tools beyond the primary market leaders, specifically investigating platforms tailored for real-time monitoring, embedded analytics, and user-led data exploration. I explored Grafana for its strengths in real-time system monitoring (DevOps/MLOps), Looker Studio for its accessibility to non-technical users within the Google Cloud ecosystem, and Metabase for its focus on self-service analytics. I also gained an introduction to Sisense and Qlik Sense, noting their capabilities in enterprise-grade predictive analytics and embedded reporting. Finally, I improved my insight into the advantages of using Python libraries like Dash, Streamlit, and Panel to build local, secure dashboards without the need for third-party cloud hosting.

This has given me a better understanding of how to match a specific business need—such as high-frequency data monitoring or customer-facing embedded reports—with the most appropriate technical platform. Allows me to understand the difference between open-source self-hosted solutions (which offer maximum data privacy) and managed cloud platforms (which offer ease of use but require strict data governance). These skills enhance my ability to provide resourceful recommendations to my organization, ensuring that sensitive business data is handled securely while remaining accessible to the relevant stakeholders.

I learned the foundational architecture of the Panel library, a member of the HoloViz ecosystem designed for creating interactive web applications and dashboards directly from Python. I explored the three core components: Widgets (for user input control), Panes (for displaying diverse media like plots, images, and Markdown), and Panels (layout containers like Row, Column, Tabs, and GridSpec). I gained an introduction to the workflow of connecting these components using custom functions to create reactive dashboards, and I improved my insight into how Panel extends the capabilities of ipywidgets to offer a more robust, server-ready deployment environment.

This has given me a better understanding of the modular nature of dashboard construction, where individual visual objects are wrapped in functions to enable dynamic updates. Allows me to understand the difference between static data exploration and building a persistent, browser-based tool that allows stakeholders to filter and manipulate data in real-time. These skills, combined with my previous training in pn.rx and templates, enhance my ability to design highly structured interfaces that are both functional in a Jupyter Notebook and deployable as standalone secure web servers.

I learned the fundamental role of Business Intelligence (BI) tools, specifically focusing on the market leaders Power BI and Tableau. I explored the broader definition of Business Intelligence as an umbrella term encompassing data mining, performance benchmarking, and descriptive analytics. I gained an introduction to the specific ecosystems supporting these tools—noting Power BI's integration within the Microsoft Power Platform and Tableau's connection to the Salesforce ecosystem—and improved my insight into the licensing and privacy considerations of their free versions, such as Power BI Desktop and the open-access nature of Tableau Public.

This has given me a better understanding of how organizational infrastructure often dictates the choice of analytical software. Allows me to understand the difference between using a specialized BI platform for enterprise-wide reporting versus using standalone libraries for bespoke analysis. These skills enhance my readiness to evaluate the tools available within my own workplace and provide me with the foundational knowledge needed to advocate for appropriate software access to make my data analysis more accessible to non-technical stakeholders.

I learned the fundamental definition and purpose of data dashboards as centralized tools for consolidating disparate data sources into a single, cohesive interface. I explored the hierarchical classification of dashboards, gaining an introduction to Strategic (high-level KPIs), Tactical/Analytical (mid-level performance tracking), and Operational (real-time process monitoring) subtypes. I also improved my insight into the ecosystem of dashboarding software—including Python's Panel library, Power BI, and Tableau—and how these tools serve both technical and non-technical stakeholders by translating complex data into actionable business narratives.

This has given me a better understanding of the functional role dashboards play in answering the "what," "why," and "what next" of business performance. Allows me to understand the difference between a single data visualization (a focused view of a specific metric) and a comprehensive dashboard (a collection of related visuals that explain a broader business situation). These skills enhance my ability to align my technical development with organizational needs, ensuring I select the right dashboard type and software to help stakeholders make informed, data-driven decisions.

I learned how to programmatically extract text strings from HTML tags and manage nested elements (such as span tags within h1 tags) using separators to maintain data integrity. I did this by completing a technical lesson on Web Scraping using the Python library BeautifulSoup, specifically focusing on the .get_text() method. This method combines the text from parent and child tags into a single string.

This has improved my confidence in handling unstructured data sources. I can now apply these techniques to parse information from web pages, allowing me to gather and clean external data for analysis where structured datasets are not readily available in the workplace. This makes it much easier to cleanly collect and analyse textual data from web pages for tasks like data analysis.
Competencies: K3 B4 K11 S14 S15 B2

Description:
I learned that BeautifulSoup’s .select() method allows us to use CSS selectors—just like in a .css file—to locate elements in the HTML. This is a powerful alternative to .find() and .find_all().

Key takeaways:

Use class selectors with a period (.) to select elements by class, e.g., .recipeLink.

Use ID selectors with a hash (#) to select elements by ID, e.g., #selected.

Use nested selectors to target elements within other elements, e.g., .recipeLink > a to select all links inside .recipeLink divs.

Loops can be combined with .select() to follow links and create new soup objects for each linked page.

This approach makes scraping more intuitive when we already understand CSS, and it enables targeting complex HTML structures efficiently.
Competencies: B2 S15 S14 K11 K3 B4

Description:
I learned that BeautifulSoup offers two primary methods for locating elements: .find() and .find_all(). .find() returns the first occurrence of a tag, while .find_all() returns a list of all matching tags. Both methods are much more flexible than directly accessing tags with the soup object.

I explored advanced selection techniques:

Using regular expressions to match patterns in tag names, such as "h[1-9]" for headings or "[ou]l" for  and  lists.

Passing lists of tags to find multiple types of elements at once.

Matching elements by attributes using dictionaries, such as classes or IDs.

Creating custom functions to implement more complex selection logic, allowing precise extraction of elements based on multiple conditions.

This is powerful because it allows targeted data extraction, even from complex or inconsistent HTML structures.
Competencies: B2 K3 S15 S14 B4 K11

Description:
I learned how to traverse the HTML structure of a web page using BeautifulSoup. By accessing tags directly (e.g., soup.h1), I can retrieve specific elements. I practiced navigating down the tree using .children to loop through child elements, such as the  tags in a recipe list. I also explored navigating up the tree using .parents, which allows me to see the container tags that hold a particular element, such as , , and .

This skill is essential for extracting structured information from web pages, especially when data is nested or hierarchically organized. Understanding both child and parent relationships enables me to pinpoint relevant content efficiently.
Competencies: S14 K3 K11 S15 B2 B4

Description:
I learned how BeautifulSoup parses HTML into structured objects, specifically Tags and NavigableStrings. Tags correspond to the HTML elements themselves, and I practiced accessing the first occurrence of a tag using the BeautifulSoup object. I also learned to retrieve the tag’s name with .name and its attributes as a dictionary using .attrs.

I gained an understanding of NavigableStrings, which represent the text contained within tags, and learned to extract the string using .string. This allows me to separate HTML structure from the actual content, enabling more precise data extraction.

This learning improves my ability to extract specific elements and content from web pages accurately, which is essential for turning web data into structured formats suitable for analysis.

I learned how to use Python’s BeautifulSoup library to parse HTML and extract specific elements from a webpage. This included converting raw HTML content into a BeautifulSoup object, which allows structured navigation of tags and elements.

I gained insight into different parser options, such as "html.parser", "lxml", and "html5lib", and why "html.parser" is a suitable choice for most basic HTML parsing tasks. I practised creating a BeautifulSoup object from both a local HTML file and content retrieved via a requests HTTP GET request.

This learning allows me to systematically extract relevant data from web pages, rather than working with messy raw HTML. It enhances my ability to transform web-based information into structured formats like DataFrames for analysis, making web scraping more efficient and actionable in a workplace context.
Competencies: B4 K10 K11 S8 S14 S15 B2 K3

Description:
I learned how to use Python’s requests library to send an HTTP GET request and retrieve the HTML content of a webpage. This introduced me to the concept of making programmatic requests to a server and receiving structured data in response.

I gained insight into the difference between webpage.text and webpage.content, understanding how to access the HTML as a string versus raw content. I also practised retrieving HTML from a local file to simulate real-world web requests safely, without overloading external servers.

This learning allows me to programmatically acquire web-based data, which is essential as a first step in web scraping or extracting structured information for analysis in a workplace context.
Competencies: S15 B2 B4 S8 K3 K10 K11

Description:
I learned the importance of following ethical and legal guidelines when scraping data from websites. This included understanding the need to review a website’s Terms and Conditions and respecting restrictions on how scraped data can be used, particularly around non-commercial use.

I gained an improved understanding of how excessive or poorly managed requests can negatively impact a website’s performance. I learned best-practice guidance for rate limiting requests to avoid placing unnecessary load on servers.

This lesson also highlighted how changes in website structure can affect scraping code, reinforcing the need to monitor and maintain data collection processes over time.

In a workplace context, this learning supports my ability to source external data responsibly, minimise operational and reputational risk, and ensure that data acquisition methods remain compliant, sustainable, and respectful of third-party systems.
Competencies: S1 K15 S8 S15 B1 B7 K3 K2 K1

Description:
I learned how to use Chrome Developer Tools to inspect and explore the structure and behaviour of modern web pages. This introduced me to examining HTML and CSS through the Elements tab and understanding how the Document Object Model (DOM) represents webpage structure.

I gained insight into how JavaScript can be inspected and tested in real time using the Console, and how browser error messages can be used to identify issues with scripts or missing resources.

I also learned how to use the Network tab to view metadata about web requests, including request methods, status codes, resource types, file sizes, and load times. This improved my understanding of how web pages request and receive data from servers.

In a workplace context, this learning supports my ability to identify where data on a webpage originates, assess how it is retrieved, and troubleshoot access issues, which is essential when sourcing data for analysis or preparing to scrape data responsibly and efficiently.
Competencies: S12 S8 K11 K10 K7 K3 B4 B2 S15

Description:
I learned how HTTP underpins communication between a client and a server, and how HTTP requests and responses are structured, including request lines, headers, optional bodies, and status codes.

This lesson gave me a clear introduction to the different HTTP request methods (such as GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE) and when each should be used. I also learned how a browser resolves a URL using DNS, establishes a TCP connection, and sends an HTTP request to retrieve a resource.

I gained an improved understanding of the difference between HTTP and HTTPS, including how HTTPS uses SSL/TLS encryption to protect data in transit and verify server identity. This has increased my awareness of security considerations when accessing or collecting data from the web.

In a workplace context, this learning supports my ability to understand how data is retrieved from web sources or APIs, helping me make informed decisions when sourcing external data for analysis and ensuring that data is accessed securely and appropriately.
Competencies: S15 K1 K2 K3 K10 K15 S1 S8 B1 B4

Description:
I learned how Python uses a default string representation when printing objects, and why this output is often not helpful for debugging or understanding program state.

This lesson introduced me to the __repr__() dunder method and how it can be implemented to define a meaningful, human-readable string representation of an object. I learned that __repr__() must return a string and can use instance variables to represent the object clearly.

This has improved my confidence in debugging object-oriented code. I am now able to customise how objects are displayed during logging or testing, making it easier to trace values and understand program behaviour.

In a workplace context, this learning supports clearer debugging and more readable outputs when working with analytical objects, improving maintainability and collaboration when reviewing code or troubleshooting issues.
Competencies: B5 K3 K6 K11 S12 S15 B4

Description:
I learned that everything in Python is treated as an object, including built-in data types such as lists, strings, and dictionaries. This gave me an introduction to Python’s object model and how user-defined and built-in objects share common behaviours.

I learned how attributes can be added to objects dynamically after instantiation, and how the dir() function can be used to inspect an object’s available attributes and methods at runtime. This improved my understanding of how Python automatically provides internal attributes and methods through dunder methods.

This has given me a better understanding of how to explore unfamiliar objects and data structures programmatically. I am now more confident in investigating the capabilities of objects when working with new libraries or datasets.

In a workplace context, this learning supports my ability to quickly understand and work with new data types, tools, or APIs by inspecting their attributes and methods, reducing reliance on trial-and-error or incomplete documentation.
Competencies: K6 K11 S4 B2 S15 B4 K3

Description:
I learned how constructors can be used to create instance variables at the point an object is created, ensuring that each object is initialised with a consistent and rigid data structure.

This improved my understanding of why objects can be more appropriate than dictionaries when modelling structured data, particularly when the data must always follow the same format. I learned how instance variables can be combined with class variables inside methods to produce meaningful outputs.

This has given me a better understanding of how object-oriented design supports cleaner, more maintainable code. I am now more confident in creating classes that represent real-world entities, where data and related logic are encapsulated together.

In a workplace context, this learning supports my ability to model structured analytical entities (such as records or reference data) and apply consistent transformations, which is useful when building repeatable data processing or feature engineering logic.
Competencies: K6 K3 S15 B2 B4 S4 K11 K10

Description:
I learned how both instance variables and class variables are treated as attributes in Python, and how attempting to access a non-existent attribute results in an AttributeError.

This introduced me to safer ways of working with object attributes using the built-in hasattr() and getattr() functions. I learned how to check whether an attribute exists before using it, and how to supply default values to prevent runtime errors when an attribute may not be present.

This has improved my confidence in writing more defensive and resilient code. I am now able to safely interact with objects that may not always have consistent attributes, reducing the risk of errors during execution.

In a workplace context, this learning supports my ability to work with dynamic or partially complete data objects, for example when handling optional fields or inconsistent records within data analysis workflows.
Competencies: K8 S4 S6 B6 B5 S15 K3 K6 K11

Description:
I learned the distinction between a class definition and individual object instances, specifically how instance variables store data that is unique to each object rather than shared across the class.

This improved my understanding of how multiple objects created from the same class can hold different values while still using the same structure and behaviour. I learned how instance variables are dynamically assigned to objects and accessed using attribute notation.

This has given me a clearer understanding of object state and how data is encapsulated at an object level. I am now more confident in designing classes where each object represents a unique record or entity, with its own associated data.

In a workplace context, this supports my ability to model real-world data entities in code, such as representing individual customers, records, or observations, which is directly applicable when building reusable data analysis components.
Competencies: S4 K3 K4 K6 K11 S15 B3 B4

Description:
I learned how special “dunder” methods in Python behave differently from regular methods, with a specific focus on the __init__() constructor and its role in object instantiation.

This gave me an introduction to how objects are automatically initialised when a class is instantiated, and how parameters can be passed into the constructor to configure an object at creation time. I learned that the constructor can also be used to validate inputs before applying logic, such as checking data types.

This has improved my understanding of how object state and behaviour are set up consistently and safely when working with classes. I am now more confident in designing classes that initialise required values and enforce basic validation rules at the point of creation.

In a workplace context, this learning supports my ability to build more robust and reusable analytical code, for example by creating data-processing objects that initialise with validated inputs, reducing the risk of downstream errors in analysis pipelines.
Competencies: K11 B4 B2 S15 S4 K3 K6

Description:
I learned how methods within a Python class can accept parameters in addition to self, and how self is implicitly passed when a method is called on an object.

This introduced me to how object instances access class attributes and use them within methods, specifically how a method can combine an input argument with stored class-level data to produce a calculated output.

This has given me a better understanding of how to structure reusable and modular code, and how objects encapsulate both data and behaviour. I am now more confident in defining simple classes and methods that perform calculations based on user input.

In a workplace context, this learning supports my ability to build cleaner, reusable analytical code (for example, utility classes for transformations or calculations), improving maintainability and consistency within data analysis workflows.
Competencies: B4 B2 S15 S4 K11 K6 K3

Description:
In the workshop, I learned how to move beyond basic plotting to fully customise visual outputs by modifying axes, labels, annotations, and colour palettes to suit the domain context. I also developed a deep insight into advanced visualisation techniques using Python libraries (Pandas, Matplotlib, and Seaborn). I gained an introduction to creating complex multi-plot grids using FacetGrid and how to visualise statistical relationships through correlation heatmaps and regression plots. This has enhanced my ability to transform raw data into accessible insights and allows me to select the most appropriate visualisation technique to effectively communicate data stories to stakeholders.

I plan to immediately apply these skills to build a performance dashboard for my team’s recurring 6-weekly task. This training has improved my confidence in approaching this task; I plan to use visualisation to explicitly demonstrate how changes to sub-stages have impacted run times over different cohorts. In particular the detailed Further Customisations gave me insights into the breadth of options available, and insight into the level of detail required when approaching these problems. This will allow me to uncover unexpected insights and communicate efficiency improvements to stakeholders more effectively.

I learned how Seaborn simplifies the visualisation of statistical relationships through functions such as regplot() for linear regression, heatmap() for correlation matrices, and catplot() for grouped numerical data. I did this by completing reading about advanced data visualisation techniques using the Seaborn library, focusing on visualising complex numerical and multivariate data. I also explored how jointplot() and pairplot() can be used to analyse multiple relationships between variables simultaneously.
This reading deepened my understanding of how visualisation supports statistical analysis - particularly in identifying trends, relationships, and outliers. I also gained practical insights into when to use different plot types based on the structure of the dataset and the analytical goal, for example using regression plots to explore predictive relationships or heatmaps to highlight correlations across large datasets.
The session enhanced both my technical skills and analytical thinking, helping me to communicate complex data more effectively to technical and non-technical audiences alike.

I learned how effective use of labels, colours, and annotations improves both the clarity and accessibility of visualisations. I did this by completing reading about key design principles for creating impactful data visualisations using Matplotlib. Specifically, I gained a stronger understanding of how descriptive titles, axis labels, and legends provide essential context for interpreting charts. I also learned how thoughtful colour selection - including sequential and diverging colour schemes - can enhance understanding while maintaining accessibility for colour-deficient audiences.
Additionally, I explored how annotations, such as arrows and text labels, can draw attention to key data points and trends without cluttering the visualisation.
This activity has improved my confidence in designing clear, accurate, and inclusive visualisations that communicate insights effectively. I now better appreciate how design decisions directly affect user interpretation and engagement with data.
Competencies: B7 K7 K13 K14 S5 S14 S15 B4

Description:
I learned the key differences between Python data visualisation libraries - including their levels of customisation, complexity, interactivity, and suitability for different analytical needs. I did this by I completing reading that was focused on Matplotlib, Seaborn, Plotly, Altair, and Bokeh. I gained a better understanding of when to use Matplotlib for fully customisable plots versus Seaborn for faster, aesthetically pleasing statistical visualisations. I also learned how interactive libraries such as Plotly and Bokeh can be valuable for dashboards and web-based reporting.
This activity improved my ability to select the most appropriate visualisation tool for specific data and audience requirements. It also highlighted the importance of evaluating open-source libraries for their documentation quality, maintenance, and licensing - knowledge that will help ensure reliable and ethical tool use within my organisation’s data workflows.
I now feel more confident in making informed decisions about which Python visualisation library to use depending on project complexity, interactivity needs, and presentation goals.
Competencies: K6 K7 K8 K14 K15 S15 B4

Description:
I learned how the type of data (categoric vs numeric) and the target audience both influence the choice of visualisation. I gained a clearer understanding of when to use charts such as bar charts, pie charts, histograms, scatter plots, and line plots to effectively communicate insights. I did this by reading and reflecting on the resource Choosing the Correct Visualisation.
This activity also helped me appreciate how poor visualisation choices can mislead an audience or obscure key findings, reinforcing the importance of designing visuals that are clear, accurate, and tailored to the viewer’s needs.
I now feel more confident in selecting the most appropriate visualisation techniques and tools for different datasets, and in adapting visuals for both technical and non-technical audiences. This understanding will improve how I communicate data insights within my role.

I learned how different chart types such as bar charts, boxplots, heatmaps, and violin plots can highlight distinct aspects of a dataset and influence how insights are interpreted by different audiences. I gained a stronger understanding of how visual design choices affect user experience and the clarity of communication in data analytics.
Through hands-on exercises, I applied descriptive statistical methods - including measures of central tendency and variability - to interpret data distributions and identify trends. I also improved my ability to use tools such as Pandas, Matplotlib, and Seaborn to create effective and meaningful visualisations.
This workshop has given me greater confidence in selecting appropriate visualisation techniques for various analytical goals and audiences, and in interpreting visual outputs both statistically and contextually.

I learned that pandas provides a simplified .plot() method to create scatterplots, histograms, bar charts, line charts, and boxplots directly from DataFrames, reducing the amount of code needed to visualise data. I did this by reading about Python data visualisation libraries, focusing on Matplotlib, pandas, and Seaborn, and how they can be used to explore and communicate insights from data.

I also learned that Matplotlib is the underlying library that handles plotting, and it allows fine-grained control of figure elements such as titles, axes, labels, and annotations to improve clarity and presentation. Additionally, I explored Seaborn, a high-level interface for Matplotlib, which simplifies creating attractive statistical plots, including violin plots, regression plots, and heatmaps. I now understand how to use these libraries to visualise distributions, correlations, and relationships between variables effectively.

This has improved my confidence in applying Python visualisation tools to explore datasets, interpret patterns, and present insights clearly for different audiences.

I learned that visual EDA helps identify patterns, relationships, and potential issues in data more effectively than reviewing raw numbers alone. I did this by reading about Visual Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) and the different methods for exploring data systematically using graphical techniques.

I gained insight into univariate analysis, which examines a single variable using charts such as histograms, bar charts, pie charts, line charts, Pareto charts, boxplots, and word clouds to summarise distributions and identify trends. I also learned about bivariate analysis, which examines the relationship between two variables using scatterplots, side-by-side bar charts, and boxplots, helping to detect correlations and potential cause-effect relationships.

This has improved my understanding of how to select and apply the most appropriate visualisation techniques for different types of data and analysis objectives. I can now confidently use visual EDA to explore datasets, highlight patterns, and communicate insights clearly.

I learned that correlation measures the strength and direction of linear relationships between two variables, ranging from –1 (strong negative) to +1 (strong positive), with values near 0 indicating weak or no correlation. I did this by reading about key statistical concepts that support interpreting data and visualisations: correlation, normal distribution, skewness, and outliers.

I also learned about normal distributions (bell curves), where mean, median, and mode are equal and most data falls within predictable ranges based on standard deviation. I gained insight into skewed distributions, where asymmetry indicates positive or negative skewness, which can highlight underlying patterns or anomalies in data.

Additionally, I learned about outliers—extreme values that can distort the mean and affect statistical analysis. I now understand multiple methods to identify outliers, including sorting, visualisation (scatterplots, boxplots, histograms), the standard deviation rule, and the interquartile range (IQR) rule. This has improved my understanding of how to interpret datasets accurately and identify potential data quality issues before analysis.

I learned that datasets with nearly identical summary statistics—mean, standard deviation, correlation, and regression coefficients—can reveal very different patterns when plotted visually. I did this by I reading about the importance of visualising data before conducting further analysis, using Anscombe’s Quartet as an example. By exploring the four datasets, I gained insight into how outliers, non-linear trends, and categorical behaviours can dramatically influence interpretation, even when summary statistics appear consistent.

This has improved my understanding of why visualisation is a critical early step in the Data Analytics Lifecycle. I now appreciate that visualising data helps identify errors, understand data structure, and uncover insights that numeric summaries alone might obscure, reinforcing the value of iterative analysis and careful exploration before modelling.

I learned how breaking down complex problems into structured stages—understand the problem, collect data, pre-process/clean/mine, visualise, pick a model, train, and evaluate & optimise—supports efficient, rigorous, and repeatable analysis. I did this by reading about the Data Analytics Lifecycle and its seven-step framework for approaching analytics projects.

I gained insight into how following the lifecycle helps manage time and costs, ensures data quality and validity at each step, and allows analytics processes to scale across teams. I now understand how each stage contributes to producing accurate, actionable insights, from defining the problem and identifying stakeholders, to collecting and cleansing relevant data, visualising patterns, selecting and training models, and optimising solutions. This has improved my confidence in planning, managing, and executing analytics projects systematically.

I learned that well-designed visualisations can clarify insights, highlight key messages, and engage the audience effectively. I did this by reading a guide on creating effective data visualisations and common mistakes to avoid. I gained insight into how to choose visual types based on the purpose—such as showing comparisons, relationships, compositions, or correlations—and how audience knowledge should guide design choices.

I also learned about best practices for colour and font selection, including using colourblind-friendly palettes, readable fonts, and consistent formatting to improve clarity and comprehension. Additionally, I learned common pitfalls to avoid, such as overloading visuals with information, misusing 3D graphics, and using inappropriate chart types like complex pie charts. This has improved my understanding of how to communicate data clearly and responsibly, and how to design visualisations that are accessible, accurate, and impactful for the intended audience.

I learned how to write data to a JSON file in Python using the json module and the 'w' argument with the open() function. I learned that opening a file with 'w' overwrites any existing content, and that the json.dump() function can be used to write Python objects, such as dictionaries or lists, to a JSON file in a structured format. This has improved my understanding of exporting hierarchical and structured data for storage or sharing, and I can now confidently save processed data in JSON format for further analysis or use in applications.

I learned how to use Python’s json module to open and read JSON files. I learned that JSON is a structured format for storing data, and the json module allows me to parse this data into Python dictionaries for analysis. This has improved my understanding of handling structured, hierarchical data formats and has enhanced my ability to work with datasets from APIs or other sources that use JSON. I can now confidently read, interpret, and process JSON data in Python.

I learned how to write data to a CSV file in Python using the open() function with the 'w' argument and the output_writer.writerow() method. I learned that opening the file with 'w' overwrites existing content, and that by combining writerow() with a for loop, I can efficiently write multiple rows of data to the file. This has improved my understanding of exporting structured data for reporting or further analysis, and I can now confidently automate the process of writing datasets to CSV files.

I learned how to read a CSV file in Python and specify the delimiter, recognising that CSV files can use a variety of characters (not just commas) to separate values. I learned how setting the correct delimiter is essential to accurately parse and interpret the data. This has improved my understanding of how to handle structured datasets from different sources, and I can now confidently read and process data files that use alternative delimiters, ensuring accurate input for analysis.

I learned how to create methods within a Python class. Methods are functions that belong to a class and define the behaviours that objects of the class can perform. I learned that the first argument of any method is always the instance calling the method, conventionally named self, and that every method must have at least this one argument. This has improved my understanding of how to organise functionality within classes, allowing objects to perform actions while maintaining access to their own data and attributes.

I learned about class variables in Python, which are variables shared across all instances of a class. I learned that a class variable is defined within the class definition, and every object of the class has access to the same variable value. I also learned that class variables can be accessed using the object.variable syntax.
