This has improved my understanding of how to manage data that should be consistent across multiple objects and has enhanced my ability to structure and maintain shared information efficiently in object-oriented programming.

I learned that a class instance is also referred to as an object, and that the process of defining classes and creating objects is known as Object-Oriented Programming (OOP). I now understand that OOP helps structure programs by representing real-world entities and their responsibilities through objects. I also learned that the type() function in Python can be used to identify the class an object belongs to, effectively performing the reverse of instantiation. This has given me a clearer understanding of how OOP organises data and behaviour in a program, improving my ability to design modular and maintainable code.

I learned that defining a class in Python only creates the blueprint for a data type — it doesn’t perform any action until it is instantiated. Instantiation involves creating an instance (or object) of the class, which allows the class’s attributes and methods to be used. I also learned that instantiating a class uses a syntax similar to calling a function. This has improved my understanding of how classes transition from abstract definitions to usable objects, helping me better grasp how data and functionality are connected in object-oriented programming.

I learned that a class in Python acts as a template for creating data types. It defines the attributes and behaviours (methods) that describe how objects of that class will store and interact with data. I learned how to define a class using the class keyword and that, according to the PEP 8 Style Guide, class names should be written in CapitalizedWords format to make them easily identifiable in code. This has improved my understanding of object-oriented programming principles and how classes help organise and structure data logically and efficiently.

I learned how to use the with statement in Python to open files as a context manager. I now understand that using with open() automatically manages the file’s opening and closing, ensuring that resources are properly released even if an error occurs during processing. This has given me a better understanding of writing cleaner, safer, and more efficient code when handling files. It has also improved my confidence in managing file operations and reducing potential errors related to unclosed files.

I learned how to write data to a file in Python using the open() function with the 'w' argument and the .write() method. I now understand that opening a file in write mode ('w') creates a new file if it doesn’t exist and overwrites any existing content if it does. This has improved my understanding of how file handling works in Python and how data can be stored or replaced within files. I can now confidently write outputs from analysis or data transformations into text files, helping me record or export results efficiently.

I learned how to use the .readlines() method in Python to iterate through the lines of a text file using a for loop. I learned that .readlines() reads all lines from a file and stores them as a list, which allows each line to be processed individually in a loop. This has improved my understanding of how data can be extracted and manipulated from text-based sources, such as logs or CSV files. I can now confidently apply iteration to handle line-by-line processing, which is useful for cleaning or analysing raw data files.

I attended a seminar hosted by the Ada Lovelace Institute on Emerging Technology and Industry Practice. The session deepened my understanding of how AI and data-driven technologies can impact society, particularly around fairness, accountability, and responsible innovation. I learned how the institute collaborates with policymakers and industry leaders to create frameworks that ensure technological advances benefit society ethically and equitably.

The seminar improved my awareness of how ethical principles, such as transparency and inclusivity, are essential in designing and deploying AI systems. I gained insight into the importance of stakeholder engagement and the role of research organisations in shaping governance and regulation for emerging technologies. This has enhanced my understanding of how ethics underpin trustworthy and responsible data use in both research and industry contexts.

I attended a presentation by the Office for National Statistics (ONS) on the decision to proceed with a full Census in 2031. I learned about the ongoing debate between maintaining a traditional census and transitioning toward a data model based primarily on administrative data sources. From the session, I gained a deeper understanding of the limitations of current administrative datasets — particularly around consistency, completeness, and timeliness — and why these challenges prevent them from fully replacing the census at this stage.

This helped me appreciate how data quality and governance influence the reliability of large-scale datasets, and how such strategic decisions balance innovation with accuracy and public trust. I now have an improved understanding of the principles of administrative data, its role in population statistics, and how it can complement rather than replace traditional data collection methods in the near term.

workplace learning: I learned how to pass an existing Spark session as a parameter into functions, allowing me to reuse the same session when moving functions to separate Python files or modules.

I now understand how this avoids the need to reinitialise Spark sessions repeatedly, reduces overhead, and ensures consistent configuration across different scripts. This has improved my confidence in creating modular, reusable code that integrates seamlessly into larger data pipelines.

Applying this method allows me to write cleaner, more maintainable functions that are portable and efficient for workplace Spark workflows.
Competencies: S15 S2 K11 K6 B2 B4

Description:
workplace learning:  I learned how to append a new row of data to an existing CSV file using Python, including using the csv module and the with open(..., 'a') mode to safely add data without overwriting existing content.

I now understand how to manage CSV files dynamically, ensuring that new data can be added incrementally while maintaining file integrity. This has improved my confidence in handling outputs from data processing tasks and maintaining organised, up-to-date datasets.

Using this method allows me to update datasets efficiently and reliably, which is essential for workflows where data is collected or processed in multiple stages.
Competencies: S15 K6 K11 S2 S14 B4

Description:
workplace learning: I learned how to update or rename the filename of a pre-existing file using Python, including using functions from the os module.

I now understand how to safely rename files programmatically, check for file existence, and avoid overwriting important data. This has improved my confidence in managing file systems and organising outputs for data projects.

Applying this method allows me to maintain structured and traceable file storage, supporting reproducible workflows and reducing errors when handling multiple files.

workplace learning:  I learned the syntax to access elements in a YAML file using Python, including loading the file with the yaml library and retrieving nested keys and values safely.

I now understand how YAML structures data hierarchically and how to navigate nested dictionaries and lists within it. This has improved my confidence in reading configuration or data files in YAML format and extracting the specific information I need for analysis or workflow automation.

Using this approach allows me to work with structured configuration and data files efficiently, ensuring I can integrate YAML data into my Python scripts reliably.

I learned how to use .items() on a dictionary to return all key-value pairs as a sequence of tuples that can be iterated over.

I now understand how this allows me to access both keys and values simultaneously for processing or transforming data, without creating a separate list. This has improved my confidence in efficiently iterating over dictionaries for analysis, mapping, or data cleaning tasks.

Using .items() enables me to manipulate or inspect dictionaries systematically, supporting reliable and structured workflows in Python.

I learned how to use .values() on a dictionary to return a dict_values view object, which is read-only but iterable.

I now understand how this method allows me to iterate over all the values in a dictionary efficiently without creating a separate list, and how it differs from a mutable list when modifications are required. This has improved my confidence in inspecting and working with dictionary data for analysis tasks.

Using .values() enables safe and efficient access to all data stored in a dictionary, supporting iteration and data processing workflows without accidentally altering the structure.

I learned how to retrieve all keys from a dictionary using the list() function to create a list of keys, or using .keys() to get a dict_keys view object that is read-only but iterable.

I now understand the difference between a mutable list of keys and the read-only dict_keys view, and when to use each approach depending on whether I need to modify the keys or just iterate over them. This has improved my confidence in efficiently exploring and working with dictionary data structures during analysis.

Using these methods allows me to inspect, iterate over, and manipulate dictionary data safely and appropriately for the task at hand.

I learned how to use the .pop() method on a dictionary to remove a key and simultaneously retrieve its value, including specifying a default value to return if the key is not present.

I now understand how this method allows me to safely remove keys without raising errors, and how it can be used to clean or restructure dictionaries dynamically during data processing. This has improved my confidence in managing Python data structures efficiently and handling missing or optional data gracefully.

Using .pop() with a default ensures that my code is robust, concise, and capable of handling variable datasets, supporting more efficient and error-tolerant analysis workflows.
Competencies: S4 S15 K6 K8 B4 B2

Description:
I learned how to use the .get() method on a dictionary to safely retrieve the value of a key, rather than using my_dict[key], which can raise a KeyError if the key is missing.

I now understand how .get() allows me to provide a default value when a key is absent, improving the robustness of my code when working with incomplete or unpredictable datasets. This has enhanced my confidence in handling real-world data reliably and reduces the risk of runtime errors during data processing.

Applying this method ensures my Python scripts are more error-tolerant and maintainable, supporting safer data analysis workflows.
Competencies: K8 S4 S15 B2 B4 K6

Description:
I learned how to safely check for the existence of a key in a dictionary using the if and in keywords before attempting to access it.

I now understand how this approach prevents runtime errors when working with missing or optional keys, ensuring that my code runs reliably even with incomplete or variable data. This has improved my confidence in handling real-world datasets where data quality may be inconsistent.

Applying this technique allows me to write more robust and error-tolerant Python code, which supports reliable data analysis workflows.
Competencies: K8 S4 S15 B2 B4 K6

Description:
I learned how to create dictionaries efficiently using dictionary comprehensions, including combining two lists into key-value pairs using zip().

I now understand how to generate dictionaries dynamically in a concise and readable way, improving my ability to manipulate data structures for analysis or preprocessing tasks. This has enhanced my confidence in writing Python code that is both efficient and maintainable.

Using dictionary comprehensions allows me to transform and organise data quickly, which supports downstream analysis or data cleaning tasks.

workplace learning:  I learned how to export specific logs or a PySpark DataFrame to a CSV file within a Cloudera session, adding a timestamp as a prefix to the filename for versioning and traceability.

I now understand how to filter or select relevant data, generate a timestamp dynamically, and save the results in a structured location within Cloudera. This has improved my ability to create reproducible outputs and maintain organised datasets for analysis or auditing purposes.

This learning enhances my confidence in producing clean, traceable data exports that can be shared or used in downstream workflows reliably.

Description:
I learned about geometry types such as points, lines, and polygons, and how to calculate weighted population centroids. I also gained insight into spatial relationships, including Exact, Basic, and Best Fit methods. I did this by attending a seminar covering the basics of geospatial data, including coordinate reference systems, map projections, and spatial data types (raster and vector).

I now have a foundational understanding of geospatial concepts, which improves my ability to interpret and analyse location-based data. This learning enhances my confidence in applying spatial reasoning to data projects and selecting appropriate methods for spatial analysis tasks.

workplace learning: I learned that while DataFrames are highly optimised for large-scale distributed computations, using lists of tuples can be more efficient for small-scale or intermediate operations where DataFrame overhead is unnecessary. I did this by exploring the efficiency of using PySpark DataFrames compared to storing data as a list of tuples for intermediate calculations.

This has improved my understanding of performance considerations in PySpark and when to choose the most appropriate data structure for a given task.

This insight allows me to optimise workflows, balance computational efficiency, and make informed decisions about data representation during analysis.

workplace learning: I learned about type hints in Python, including how to annotate function parameters and return values with expected data types.

I now understand how type hints improve code readability, help catch potential errors early, and make collaborative coding easier by clearly communicating the intended data types. This has enhanced my confidence in writing maintainable, robust Python code and supporting better teamwork in data projects.

Using type hints allows me to anticipate data issues before runtime and improves the quality and reliability of analytical code.

workplace learning: I learned how to use .withColumn() combined with when() and otherwise() in PySpark to selectively update values in a DataFrame column based on conditions. I extended this by creating a custom function that allows updating any column of the user’s choice dynamically.

I now understand how to implement conditional logic across datasets programmatically, enabling flexible data transformation without manually rewriting code for each column. This has improved my confidence in building reusable tools for cleaning, transforming, and preparing datasets efficiently.

These skills enhance my ability to automate data processing tasks and ensure consistent application of rules across large datasets.
Competencies: B2 K6 B4 K11 S4 S15

Description:
Workplace learning: I learned how to create a Spark DataFrame from multiple sources, including two CSV files, a list of tuples, and a schema definition file.

I now understand how to combine data from different sources and apply a schema to ensure the DataFrame has the correct structure and data types. This has improved my confidence in setting up data pipelines and preparing datasets for analysis while maintaining consistency and integrity across data sources.

Using these methods allows me to handle varied data inputs efficiently and ensures that subsequent analysis or transformations can be applied reliably.
Competencies: K6 K10 S15 S4 S8 B4

Description:
Workplace learning: I learned about, and applied the Spark/PySpark command select("column_name").distinct().count() to identify the number of unique values in a dataset column.

I learned how to quickly calculate the uniqueness of data entries, which is important for understanding data quality, detecting duplicates, and validating datasets. This has improved my ability to assess and clean data efficiently within workplace datasets.

Using this method has enhanced my confidence in performing exploratory data analysis and ensures I can accurately summarise key aspects of datasets before further processing or modelling.

I learned how to create and manage virtual environments in Python using pipenv, including setting specific versions of packages via the Pipfile and understanding how system PATH environment variables affect package accessibility.

I now understand how virtual environments isolate project dependencies, prevent version conflicts, and maintain reproducibility across projects. I also learned how to configure package versions explicitly, which ensures that analyses remain consistent over time.

This has improved my confidence in setting up clean, reliable Python environments for data analysis, supporting better project organisation and reproducible workflows.
Competencies: K6 S2 S15 B2 B4 K11

Description:
I explored the basics of Python’s datetime module, learning how to create, format, and manipulate dates and times.

I learned how to convert strings to datetime objects, calculate time differences, and format dates for reporting or analysis. This has improved my understanding of how time-based data is handled in Python and how to manage inconsistencies in date formats when preparing datasets.

This has enhanced my ability to clean and transform temporal data accurately, which is essential for analysing trends over time in my projects.

I learned how to import and use a custom function from another Python file within the same directory.

I now understand how Python’s module system works, including how to structure code into separate files for better organisation and reusability. I also learned how the import statement and __init__.py files help manage project directories and how this improves maintainability and collaboration in larger projects.

This has given me greater confidence in writing modular, reusable code and setting up my project directories correctly for future analytical work.

I completed a learning activity covering the basics of Matplotlib for data visualisation and explored how to use Python’s decimal module to address floating-point precision errors.

I learned how to create simple plots using Matplotlib, including line and bar charts, and how to label and style visual elements clearly. I also learned why floating-point errors occur in numerical calculations and how the decimal library ensures greater accuracy when working with financial or high-precision data.

This has improved my confidence in presenting data visually and ensures I can handle numerical data accurately during analysis and reporting.

Description:
I completed a learning activity on string manipulation in Python, specifically focusing on the use of replace(), find(), and format() functions, including formatting with keyword arguments.

I learned how to search for and modify text within strings efficiently, and how to dynamically insert variables into text templates using both positional and keyword-based formatting.

This has given me a clearer understanding of how text data can be processed and prepared for analysis. I now feel more confident in cleaning and transforming unstructured text data to make it usable for analysis workflows.

Learning activity: I learned how to create a class diagram that represents several classes, including their methods, attributes, data types, visibility (public/private), and default values. This expanded my understanding of object-oriented design, showing me how to formally visualise the relationships and structure of multiple classes in a system. I now have the skills to document class architecture clearly, making it easier to plan, communicate, and maintain complex codebases. This goes beyond just defining single classes or methods, as it provides a structured overview of multiple classes and their interactions.

Description: Learning activity: I learned about the basic structure of classes in programming, including how to define attributes (data stored within a class) and methods (functions associated with the class). This gave me a better understanding of object-oriented principles and how to organise code in a reusable and logical way. I now feel confident in creating simple classes to structure data and functionality, which enhances my ability to write cleaner, more maintainable code for analysis tasks.

This has enhanced my understanding of how to organise and present analysis work effectively, providing me with tools to communicate complex data structures and workflows to stakeholders.

Description: I learned how to use groupby and agg together in pandas to summarise datasets and calculate statistics across groups. This gave me a clearer understanding of how to extract insights by applying aggregation functions such as mean, sum, min, and max to grouped data. I also discovered how functions like count and size handle missing values differently, which improved my awareness of managing data quality. Additionally, I practiced merging aggregated tables back into a detailed DataFrame, which enriched the dataset with summary insights. This has strengthened my ability to implement key stages of the data lifecycle, apply statistical methodologies, and select the most effective tools for analysing and presenting grouped data.
Competencies: K8 S10 K3 K10 K11 K13 S2 S4 S13 S14 S15 B3 B4 B5

Description: I learned how to identify and resolve data quality issues within datasets, including handling missing values (NaN), correcting inconsistent or incorrect entries, and adjusting data types. I also gained experience in combining datasets using joins and concatenation in pandas, which improved my understanding of how different data structures and database design principles affect analysis. This has given me a stronger grasp of the steps in the data analysis lifecycle, from cleaning and transforming data to preparing it for deeper analysis. I now feel more confident in choosing the appropriate methods and tools to process data effectively, which enhances my ability to carry out reliable analysis.
Competencies: B3 B4 B5 K11 K10 K8 K6 K3 S2 S4 S8 S15

Description:
I learned how to apply Python data structures, including lists and tuples, alongside logical rules, loops, and conditionals, to solve practical data processing problems. Specifically, I learned how to implement conditional logic and iteration to manage real-world scenarios, such as handling concert entry rules based on age and capacity limits.

I learned this through hands-on exercises where I transformed raw data, applied data processing rules, and produced categorised outputs. I also learned new techniques for debugging code efficiently and structuring loops and conditionals to ensure accurate filtering, classification, and organisation of data.

This activity enhanced my ability to apply logical and analytical thinking in coding tasks, improved my confidence in selecting and using appropriate programming tools, and gave me practical skills to handle structured data problems more effectively.

I learned how to use Python data structures - including lists, sets, and dictionaries - in more advanced ways to support practical data analysis tasks. I learned how to transform data between types, such as creating dictionaries from lists, and how to apply sets to identify unique elements in a dataset.

I learned how to implement error-handling functions to ensure input data is of the correct type, which gave me new knowledge about proactively identifying and mitigating potential data quality issues. I also learned analytical techniques such as counting duplicates and grouping data alphabetically, which are foundational for data mining and pattern recognition.

I learned how to apply stages of the data analysis lifecycle in practice, including data processing, classification, and interpretation. For example, I categorised names into dictionary keys based on their first letter, which reinforced my understanding of classification principles. I also gained new skills in creating reusable functions and organising code efficiently within Jupyter Notebook.

This activity strengthened my logical and analytical thinking, improved my ability to structure and analyse datasets, and enhanced my resilience in problem-solving through trial and error. The outputs I created - structured dictionaries and reusable functions - provide practical tools that I can apply to future datasets.
Competencies: S12 S13 S14 S15 B1 B2 K11 S2 S3 S4 S6 B6 B5 B4 B3 K6 K8

Description:
I learned how to work with files and key Python modules - os, json, and math - to support automation, data processing, and problem-solving.

I learned how to perform file operations in Python, including opening, reading, writing, and closing files, which are essential for working with persistent data. I also learned how to use the os module to interact with the operating system, including navigating file paths, creating or removing directories, and managing environment variables.

I learned how to use the json module to parse JSON strings into dictionaries and lists, and to serialise Python objects back into JSON. These skills are directly applicable to handling structured data and working with APIs in data engineering contexts.

Finally, I learned how to use the math module to access advanced mathematical functions, such as trigonometry, logarithms, and constants like pi and e, broadening my ability to perform complex analytical and computational tasks in Python.

This activity enhanced my practical knowledge of Python’s standard library and improved my ability to use these tools to manipulate data, automate tasks, and solve analytical problems."

I learned how to combine lists and use loops in Python to manipulate and process data in more advanced ways. Specifically, I learned how to apply for and while loops with range() and conditional logic to generate new outputs from multiple lists, and how to structure while loops safely to avoid infinite iterations.

I learned this through hands-on exercises where I applied these techniques to perform operations on lists, combining data and generating outputs based on conditions. This activity gave me new insight into controlling program flow, applying logical structures effectively, and preparing datasets for further analysis or transformation in Python.

This activity strengthened my ability to write reliable, efficient code that can handle more complex data processing tasks and improved my confidence in applying Python loops and list operations in real-world scenarios.

learning activity: I learned how to translate transformed data from an ETL pipeline into structured database tables using SQL, ensuring alignment with organisational database standards. Specifically, I learned how to define table schemas that include field names and data types, to enforce data integrity. I also learned how to validate SQL scripts against expected schema definitions and troubleshoot errors such as datatype mismatches or missing columns.

I learned this by working on the final stage of an ETL pipeline, where I created and tested database tables to ensure the processed data could be reliably loaded, queried, and used for downstream reporting and analysis. This exercise gave me practical experience in integrating SQL with ETL processes and reinforced the importance of consistency between transformed data and final database schemas.

This activity enhanced my understanding of relational database principles, schema enforcement, and data modelling, and improved my ability to produce high-quality, structured outputs that support accurate and reliable analytics.

I learned how to use nested loops and list comprehensions in Python to handle complex data processing tasks more efficiently. Specifically, I learned how to iterate over multi-dimensional data structures, such as lists of lists, to access and manipulate elements within sublists, and how to apply conditional logic within list comprehensions to filter or transform data in a single line.

I learned this by practicing nested loops and constructing list comprehensions for tasks that required comparing, transforming, or aggregating data across multiple levels. This exercise helped me understand the differences between standard loops and list comprehensions in terms of readability, execution efficiency, and suitability for different types of data manipulation tasks.

Through this activity, I strengthened my logical thinking, problem-solving skills, and ability to write clean, efficient code, enhancing my capability to manipulate, analyse, and prepare real-world datasets.

Learning Activity: I learned how the sequence of transformations in an ETL pipeline affects the application of variable datatypes and the reliability of downstream data ingestion. Specifically, I discovered that datatype transformations must be applied before other data-cleaning steps, such as trimming whitespace, and only to relevant variables to prevent inconsistencies.

I learned this by investigating an ETL pipeline issue where one table’s variable datatypes were not being applied correctly, causing errors downstream. I reviewed the SQL script and schema definitions to verify the intended datatypes and traced the problem to the order of transformations in the pipeline. By reordering the steps so that datatype transformations occurred first, the pipeline successfully enforced the correct datatypes and passed data reliably to downstream processes.

This activity reinforced my understanding of how each step in an ETL workflow depends on the previous one, and how misordering can create subtle but critical data issues. It also emphasized the importance of systematic debugging and testing to identify the true root cause rather than just addressing the symptoms.

I learned about Python’s design philosophy, the decisions that shaped its development, and the challenges faced during major transitions such as from Python 2 to Python 3. I gained insight into the role of the community in driving Python’s evolution, collaborative development practices, and the importance of backward compatibility.

I learned this by watching Python: The Documentary, which traces the language’s history from its origins as a side project to becoming one of the world’s most widely used programming languages. The documentary included interviews with Guido van Rossum and other key contributors, helping me understand how Python has developed over time and the factors that have influenced its adoption and growth.

As part of a learning activity, I developed a deeper understanding of ETL pipeline integrity and learned the importance of verifying that data transformations produce consistent results. Using PySpark to compare staged and promoted tables, I gained new knowledge about how table structures, data types, and row counts must align across stages to ensure reliability.

I also learned how the sequence of transformations impacts the final output and how misalignment at one step can create subtle errors downstream. This exercise enhanced my analytical skills in systematically auditing datasets and improved my understanding of reproducibility and traceability in automated pipelines.

Overall, this activity increased my understanding of pipeline validation, reinforced best practices for data quality, and highlighted how programming can be applied not only for processing but also for verification and quality assurance.
Competencies: S15 B5 B2 S4 K6 S2

Description: Learned how variable scope limits access outside of a function. Gained insight into using return statements to pass values back and an introduction to lambda functions for concise, single-line operations. This has improved my understanding of how functions control data flow and allows me to write more modular, efficient, and reusable code.

Learned how to use parameters and arguments in functions, including multiple parameters, positional arguments, keyword arguments, and default values. Gained insight into how these affect function behavior and execution flow. This has improved my understanding of writing adaptable and reusable code, and allows me to structure functions more efficiently while reducing the risk of errors.

Learned how to declare and call functions in my chosen programming environment. Gained an understanding of how whitespace affects code readability and execution flow. This has improved my confidence in structuring code logically, and I now have a better understanding of how program execution follows a defined sequence, allowing me to write more efficient and error-free functions.

During this session, I deepened my knowledge of fundamental Python data structures - including lists, sets, and dictionaries - and applied these to practical data analysis tasks. I wrote functions to transform data between types, such as creating dictionaries from lists, which reinforced my understanding of data organisation and manipulation.

A key focus was data quality and risk mitigation. I implemented an error_catching function to ensure input data was of the correct type, demonstrating how to identify potential issues and handle them proactively. I also explored analytical techniques, including counting duplicates and grouping data alphabetically, which are foundational forms of data mining and pattern recognition.

The session gave me hands-on experience in implementing stages of the data analysis lifecycle, particularly data processing, classification, and interpretation. I applied classification principles by categorising names into dictionary keys based on their first letter, and used sets to identify unique elements, improving my ability to analyse and structure datasets effectively.

Working in the Jupyter Notebook environment, I practised creating reusable functions and organising code for clarity and efficiency. The workshop encouraged collaboration and professional communication, allowing discussion and idea-sharing with peers and tutors. Through trial and error, I applied logical and analytical thinking, debugged errors, and built robust solutions, enhancing both my technical skills and resilience in problem-solving.

Outputs from this session included structured dictionaries representing organised and validated data, and functions capable of automating routine analytical tasks—tools that can be applied to a range of datasets in future work.

Description:
In this entry, I learned how to formalise technical notes into a structured project scoping document that aligns with apprenticeship and organisational requirements. This was the first time I translated a technical task I had already completed (running supplier data through a pipeline) into a structured project proposal format.

Through this, I gained experience in:

Framing a technical workflow as a business problem - explaining why data standardisation matters for efficiency, trust, and decision-making.

Defining candidate datasets and preprocessing needs - describing data sources, formats, and the steps needed to make them usable.

Outlining proposed methods and outputs - justifying tool choices (e.g., PySpark, Hive SQL, Cloudera) and clarifying what the project will deliver (tables, logs, and a consolidated data dictionary).

Measuring business impact - identifying success criteria such as reduced manual effort, improved accuracy, and faster issue resolution.

Recognising risks and limitations - such as supplier data issues, air-gapped environment restrictions, or the need for stakeholder buy-in.

This exercise improved my ability to think beyond the technical detail and instead connect the work to organisational goals, risks, and impact. It also gave me practice in writing for stakeholders by making the project accessible to non-technical readers, while still showing the depth of the technical plan.

I now understand how relative imports help maintain modular project structure, avoid hard-coded paths, and make code more reusable and easier to maintain. This has improved my ability to organise PySpark projects logically, strengthened my understanding of package structure, and given me confidence in linking configuration files across multiple modules efficiently.

I learned how to set up a YAML/YML file, including proper file structure and syntax. I learned how to link the YAML file to other files and databases, and how to use it as an ETL schema file to define data sources, transformations, and outputs. I also explored generating outputs for Hive tables from the YAML configuration. This has improved my understanding of structuring configuration files for ETL workflows, strengthened my ability to connect data sources programmatically, and given me confidence in using YAML to automate and standardise data pipelines."
Competencies: B4 B5 K6 K3 S15 S4

Description: I learned what a YAML/YML file is — a human-readable data serialization format often used for configuration, data exchange, or defining structured information. I now understand that YAML files can be used to store configuration settings for applications, define schemas, manage deployment pipelines, or structure metadata in a readable format. This has improved my understanding of how structured configuration files can support reproducible workflows and given me confidence in using YAML for organising data and application settings.

I learned about types of cardinality in relationships between database tables: One-to-One, One-to-Many, and Many-to-Many. I also learned about different types of SQL joins — Left, Right, Inner, and Full Outer — and how they affect the resulting dataset when combining tables. This has improved my understanding of how data is related across tables, strengthened my ability to design queries that correctly combine information, and given me confidence in selecting the appropriate join type depending on the analysis required.

I learned key Python programming constructs, including Sequencing, Selection, and Iteration. I explored the 'Don't Repeat Yourself' (DRY) principle, which encourages writing reusable code to avoid duplication. I also learned the difference between for and while loops: for loops iterate over a list or iterable, whereas while loops continue until a specified condition is no longer met. This has improved my understanding of control flow and iteration in Python, strengthened my ability to write efficient, reusable code, and given me confidence in selecting the most appropriate loop construct depending on the task.

I gained an initial understanding of dirty data and the six data quality dimensions: Completeness, Uniqueness, Timeliness, Validity, Accuracy, and Consistency. I learned the options available when an error is found, including accepting, rejecting, correcting, or applying a default value. I also explored the difference between functional requirements (specific demands) and non-functional requirements (performance characteristics), and the distinction between explicit knowledge (easily articulated) and tacit knowledge (experience-based and subjective). This has improved my understanding of assessing data quality, managing errors, and recognising different types of knowledge relevant to data projects.
Competencies: S6 K8 K9 B4 S14 K3

Description: I learned about key data legislation, including GDPR (EU), CalOPPA and CCPA (US), HIPAA, and GBLA. I now understand the roles of data controllers (decision makers) and data processors (who act on behalf of controllers). I also learned the concept of consent — that it must be understandable, purpose-driven, and time-bound — and explored the seven key GDPR principles. Additionally, I learned practical questions to ask about data, such as its origin, whether consent has been obtained, and whether processes are being documented. This has improved my awareness of legal requirements for handling personal data and strengthened my ability to ensure compliance in data projects.

I learned that ethics also applies to machine learning, including the challenge of programming automated systems to make ethical decisions without harming people. I learned how ML systems can reinforce real-world biases if not carefully designed. I explored the OECD AI ethics principles, which emphasise respecting humans, respecting the planet, implementing safeguards, being transparent, robust, and accountable. I also learned how companies can act ethically when releasing products with unknown repercussions, such as the limited release of GPT-2 before understanding its potential misuse for spam. This has improved my awareness of ethical considerations in AI development and strengthened my ability to critically evaluate the implications of deploying machine learning systems.
Competencies: K1 B6 B4 S12 K14 K8

Description: I learned the definitions of personal data, including information that is directly or indirectly identifiable and sensitive. I also learned the difference between anonymised and pseudonymised data: anonymised data cannot be traced back to an individual, whereas pseudonymised data can be re-identified with additional information. This has improved my understanding of data privacy principles and given me a clearer framework for handling sensitive data responsibly in compliance with regulations.

I learned about how organisations acquire and use datasets to create value. I now understand that building valuable data takes time, that new datasets can enhance the value of existing datasets, and that proprietary datasets can provide a competitive advantage. I also learned what an organisation needs to be successful with data: infrastructure that allows easy access to the right data, compliance with data privacy and regulatory standards, skilled talent, and a culture that encourages innovation. This has improved my understanding of the strategic, technical, and cultural factors necessary for effective data use and informed my perspective on data-driven decision-making.
Competencies: K1 B7 B2 K2 K4 K9 S12

Description: I learned about the concept of big data, including the 5 V’s: Volume, Variety, Velocity, Veracity, and Value. I also explored the factors that have contributed to the growth of big data, such as decreasing CPU and storage costs, the increasing volume of data being logged, and reduced data transfer costs. Additionally, I learned what a regression problem is in machine learning — predicting a continuous numerical outcome based on input variables. This has improved my understanding of the big data landscape, why it has expanded rapidly, and given me a foundational understanding of supervised learning problems like regression.
Competencies: K14 B4 S15 K4 K6 K13

Description: I learned how to format an ETL schema generator by combining dot notation and function arguments with my understanding of lists to programmatically create a JSON file. This allowed me to define a structured schema efficiently and ensured that the resulting JSON accurately represents the intended data layout. This has improved my ability to automate schema creation, strengthened my understanding of nested data structures, and given me confidence in applying Python programming to support ETL workflows.

I learned how to use Hue to access Amazon S3 storage and manually inspect raw data files. This allowed me to verify that the values and formats in the dataset matched the data dictionary and to identify any discrepancies. This has improved my understanding of navigating cloud storage, strengthened my ability to perform manual data validation, and ensures I can confirm the accuracy and completeness of data before further analysis.
Competencies: S4 S15 S6 B4 K3 K6 K8

Description: I learned about the benefits of functional programming compared to object-oriented programming. I now understand that functional programming emphasises immutability, simpler testing, and predictable outputs, while object-oriented programming focuses more on encapsulation and modular structure. I also learned a shorthand way of thinking about pure functions — they can be thought of as something that could be replaced with a lookup table, as their outputs depend only on their inputs. This has given me a clearer conceptual understanding of functional programming and has improved my ability to decide when a functional approach may be preferable, particularly for data transformations.

I gained more of an introduction to object-oriented programming (OOP), particularly the challenges of testing code built with classes and objects. I learned that while OOP is often explained using real-world metaphors, it is more useful to think about it conceptually in terms of structure, behaviour, and relationships rather than forcing exact real-world comparisons. This has improved my understanding of how to approach OOP design logically, given me more awareness of where testing may be more complex, and strengthened my ability to think about modular and reusable code structures.

I learned how to use dataset metadata to design and apply regular expressions (regex) in different formats for building a data dictionary. Through this process, I developed a better understanding of regex syntax, including using multiple conditions within patterns. This has improved my ability to validate and standardise data fields efficiently, and given me more confidence in applying regex to support data quality checks and metadata-driven automation.
Competencies: K3 K6 K8 S3 S6 B5 S15 B4

Description: I learned how to use a data dictionary to retrieve the official/allowed values for key fields. Using Excel functions, I split and cleaned raw labels and built a conversion (mapping) table for downstream users. I also identified how naming inconsistencies were breaking automation and learned how to normalise these values to make joins, lookups and validation reliable. This has improved my understanding of standardising categorical data and gives me a repeatable approach I can apply to future datasets.
Competencies: S6 K3 K8 K9 S3 S7 S14 S15 B2 B4 B5

Description: I learned how to create and work with two-dimensional lists in Python. I can now define nested lists and understand how to access specific elements using row and column indexing. I also gained experience in modifying elements within a two-dimensional list and learned how to remove elements effectively. This has given me a stronger foundation in handling tabular-style data structures, improved my confidence in working with nested data, and allows me to manipulate list-based datasets more efficiently for analysis and transformation.

I learned how to use Python’s match statements with case clauses and how they compare to using multiple if/elif conditions. I now understand that match provides a cleaner and more structured way to handle multiple discrete options, whereas if/elif is more flexible for evaluating complex conditions. This has given me clearer insight into when to use each approach, which improves the readability and efficiency of my code. I now feel more confident in choosing the right control flow structure depending on the problem I need to solve.

I learned how to use conditional statements in Python, including if, else, and elif. I now understand that multiple if statements will all trigger if their conditions are true, whereas elif provides a way to evaluate exclusive conditions in sequence. This has given me a better grasp of control flow in programming, allowing me to write more efficient and predictable logic in my code. I now feel more confident in structuring decision-making processes in scripts and data workflows.

I learned how to apply Boolean operators (AND, OR, NOT) within my analysis. I now understand how these operators combine or exclude conditions when filtering datasets, which improves my ability to create more precise queries. This has given me a clearer understanding of logical expressions in data analysis, and allows me to design filters that return only the required results. It has improved my confidence in constructing conditional logic for data exploration and cleaning.

I gained a deeper understanding of how PySpark window specifications work and how they differ from groupBy operations. I learned that window functions allow row-level calculations across partitions, whereas groupBy produces aggregated results at the group level. I also explored the difference between a GroupedData object and column-based aggregation functions, and discovered that the agg() method is required when applying multiple or more complex aggregations. This has given me clearer insight into when to use each approach, improved my ability to choose the right technique depending on the analysis required, and strengthened my confidence in writing more efficient and flexible PySpark queries.
Competencies: K6 B5 B4 S15 S4 K13 K3

Description: I learned how to use PySpark’s window functions alongside key descriptive methods such as describe, summary, and count. This gave me a better understanding of how to perform aggregate operations over partitions of data and compare results across groups without losing detail at the row level. I also gained insight into how describe and summary provide different statistical perspectives of a dataset, which allows me to quickly check data quality and distribution. This has improved my confidence in handling large datasets efficiently and enhanced my ability to choose the right approach for exploratory data analysis in PySpark.
Competencies: K3 B5 B4 S15 S4 K13 K6

Description: Learned the differences between starting a new local repository using git init and adding files versus cloning an existing online repository using git clone. Also explored how to use git fetch to update local copies with changes from the remote repository. This has improved my understanding of version control workflows for both creating new projects and collaborating on existing ones, ensuring smooth integration of local and remote work.

Learned how to use exponents and modulo operations, including applying modulo to check if numbers are even or odd and to convert minutes into hours and minutes. Also learned about the += operator for incremental addition and string concatenation, as well as how to incorporate user inputs into programs. This has enhanced my foundational programming skills, enabling me to perform arithmetic operations, manipulate strings, and interact with users effectively.

Learned more about the concepts of copying data and implicit inheritance in Python, particularly how methods like drop behave in relation to these concepts. This has improved my understanding of how data manipulations affect the original datasets versus copies, helping me avoid unintended side effects during analysis.

Expanded my knowledge of Git by using commands such as status to check repository state, navigating with Vim for editing commit messages, and adding multiple comments in commits. Learned how to switch branches using checkout, perform commits to save changes, and create pull requests on GitHub for code review and collaboration. This has improved my confidence in version control workflows and collaborative development practices.

Created a function with a loop for the first time, which automated repetitive tasks and improved code efficiency. Also learned how to convert 'n/a' values to alternative values to prevent errors and ensure other functionality runs smoothly. This has enhanced my programming skills and my ability to handle missing or inconsistent data effectively.
OTJ data imported from Aptem

I enhanced and refactored my previous code to address a limitation of the pandas' rank function, which typically only works on a single column. I now know how to devise a custom approach to rank data based on three columns simultaneously, which has significantly reduced duplicate entries in my dataset. Additionally, I learned how to improve the readability and maintainability of my code by minimizing duplication, which has enhanced my problem-solving skills and my ability to write more efficient and scalable code.

Expanded my knowledge of Confluence documentation by learning how to effectively use labels for categorisation, create and assign tasks, generate task reports to track progress, and implement automations to streamline workflow management. This has enhanced my ability to organise project documentation efficiently, improve team collaboration, and maintain clear oversight of task statuses.

Learned how to use literals in PySpark to add a column with a fixed integer value rather than a value derived from a function or existing data. This has improved my ability to create new columns with constant values efficiently, which supports clearer and more flexible data transformations in PySpark workflows.

Learned how to filter columns in PySpark and remove duplicates from datasets efficiently. Also gained an understanding of PySpark's lazy loading mechanism and how using the show() method forces execution and displays the data immediately. This has improved my confidence in managing large datasets in PySpark and knowing when computations actually run.

Reaffirmed that when using indexing with slicing, the start and end positions depend on the polarity (positive or negative) of the step input, which affects how data subsets are selected. Also learned about the built-in display function and how it can be used to visually present multiple DataFrames simultaneously, improving data review and comparison. This has enhanced my understanding of precise data selection and improved my ability to present data clearly during analysis.

Created a README file with a clear and structured format to present the goals, methodology, observations, and conclusions of an analysis project. This has helped me understand how to clearly document the purpose and approach of my work so it is easy for others to follow, review, and build upon. This improves my ability to communicate my analysis process and outcomes effectively and maintain good documentation standards.

Expanded my knowledge of grouping data by learning how to assign counts within groups and rank the items based on these counts. Also learned how to merge DataFrames to compare different ranking criteria against common entries. This has improved my confidence in performing more advanced aggregations and comparisons, which supports better insights when working with grouped data.
Competencies: K3 K13 S2 S4 S10 B4

Description: Learned how to remove duplicate rows from a dataset based on a single specified column, which helps ensure the uniqueness of key values while keeping the relevant data. Also learned how to reset the index after filtering or reordering a DataFrame, which improves the structure and makes the data easier to read and work with. This has given me more confidence in cleaning and preparing datasets to maintain data quality and usability.

Learned that when importing a file into Spark and then using pandas for further manipulation, the data types can often be automatically converted to strings — for example, numeric columns may become strings and boolean values like TRUE may be converted to the string 'True'. This has given me a better understanding of the importance of checking and explicitly setting data types when moving data between Spark and pandas. This helps ensure that further analysis and calculations use the correct data types and prevents errors later in the process.

Learned how to use raw strings (r-strings) in PySpark to handle file paths more effectively by avoiding errors caused by escape characters like backslashes. This has given me a clearer understanding of how to write cleaner, more reliable file path code, which reduces mistakes when accessing files across different systems. This skill improves my confidence when setting up data input and output paths in PySpark workflows.

Learned how to set up the basics of a PySpark session and how to install and import the pandas library for data manipulation within a PySpark workflow. Also learned how to upload a CSV file to Hive and Amazon S3, which has given me a better understanding of how to store and access data using cloud and distributed systems. This has improved my confidence in setting up environments for large-scale data processing and knowing when to use different storage options.

Learned how to use a boolean mask to apply filters to a dataset, including how to match strings using wildcard-like patterns for flexible filtering. Practiced chaining methods to list all unique items in a column, count them, and convert the result into a dictionary for easier analysis. Also learned how to check whether a series or column contains any NaN values and how to combine multiple conditions using boolean masks to create more advanced filters, such as finding all rows with maximum values. This has improved my ability to write more efficient, accurate, and flexible queries when analysing data.

Reviewed an EPIDD (Extract, Process, Integrate, Deliver, and Document) document from a client and broke down my observations to identify information relevant to an ETL process. I learned how to assess what details are useful for planning extraction, transformation, and loading steps, and recognised what important information might be missing, such as data formats, source system details, or transformation rules. This has improved my confidence in reviewing documentation and ensuring all requirements for an ETL pipeline are clear and complete.
Competencies: B4 K10 S2 S7 B2 K3

Description: Learned the basics of ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) processes and how they are used to collect, clean, and prepare data from multiple sources for business use. This has given me a clearer understanding of how ETL helps ensure that data is accurate, consistent, and ready for analysis. I now know when and why ETL is needed, which improves my confidence in planning how to handle data for different business requirements.

Learned the basics of scientific notation and how it is used to simplify the representation of very large or very small numbers in data. This has helped me understand how scientific notation can reduce memory usage for different datatypes, which is useful when writing documentation and working with data structures. I now know when using scientific notation is most appropriate, which gives me more confidence in choosing suitable data formats and explaining this clearly to others.
